Positioning Regime

#2 Defensive Risk-On.

Institutions want equities but only safe equities — quality names, defensive sectors, and cash-generating businesses with strong balance sheets. Mega caps are favored while small caps and credit-sensitive businesses are actively avoided. Investors are not fleeing stocks, but they are being highly selective. This is the market's way of saying "we want exposure but don't trust the economy." The typical macro backdrop features slowing growth, elevated rates, and no recession yet — a late-cycle environment where safety is priced at a premium.

FieldRead
Equity appetiteRisk-On

BreadthVery Narrow

PositioningQuality / Defensive

Confirmation layers — independent price/volatility checks that show whether the market action confirms or contradicts the positioning regime:

LayerWhat it measuresThis weekHow it's graded
Composite scoreSingle 0–100 blend of the positioning + price/vol picture: 0.35·ES + 0.20·RTY combined positioning + 0.20·breadth percentile + 0.15·(100 − VIX percentile) + 0.10·conviction.40.8 → Neutral0–20 Panic · 20–40 Risk-Off · 40–60 Neutral · 60–80 Risk-On · 80–100 Euphoria
Layer 2 — Breadth confirmationEqual-weight ÷ cap-weight S&P 500 (^SPXEW ÷ ^GSPC): is the average stock keeping up with the mega-caps?Narrowing (mega-cap led) (pct 36.5)ratio rising > +0.5% over ~13 weeks = Broadening (healthy); falling < −0.5% = Narrowing (mega-cap led); otherwise Flat
Layer 3 — VIX positioning (COT)Combined asset-manager + leveraged-fund net-position percentile in VIX futures: are participants owning or abandoning volatility hedges?Fear — participants own volatility (defensive) (pct 94.2); Hedged Risk-On (long but nervous)VIX combined pct > 80 = Fear (defensive) · < 20 = Complacency (bullish-but-fragile) · 20–80 = Neutral. Combined with equities: ES > 70 & VIX < 30 = Confident Risk-On; ES > 70 & VIX > 70 = Hedged Risk-On; ES < 30 & VIX > 80 = Panic
Layer 4 — Positioning vs priceES combined positioning percentile vs where SPY price actually sits.AlignedES combined < 30 while SPY within 2% of its high = Bullish divergence (under-positioned into strength); ES > 70 while SPY's 4-week return ≤ 0 = Bearish divergence (crowded, no follow-through); otherwise Aligned
Regime matrixPrice + positioning cross-tab (ES/RTY positioning × breadth direction × VIX) mapped to a single named market state.Defensive Risk-One.g. Broad Risk-On, Narrow Risk-On, Defensive Risk-On, Breadth Expansion, Hedged Bull Market, Neutral, Risk-Off, Panic, Euphoria

LLM Commentary — 2026-07-14

*Model: gemini-2.5-flash*

The signal

Institutional distribution in broad indices and growth sectors is accelerating into retail buying, creating a late-cycle tension against a market structure that screams fragility. Asset Managers are lightening up in core S&P 500 (83→60) and Nasdaq 100 (60→27) positions, while retail specs crowd long (S&P 500 63→94, Dow 60→96). This comes as volatility structure sits deep in SYSTEMIC regime and the market remains in a "Fragile Concentration" state.

Why

Asset managers are actively de-risking broad index exposure. Their S&P 500 net long position has unwound significantly from 83 to 60, and their Nasdaq 100 position, after a brief mid-range spike, has now fallen from 60 to 27. Dow exposure similarly reduced from 79 to 50. This is "real money" offloading, often a precursor to broader market weakness. Leveraged funds, while maintaining elevated S&P 500 (79) and Dow (88) positions, have aggressively unwound their Russell 2000 long (56→13) and remain deeply short Nasdaq 100 (10→2). This fast-money cohort is not uniformly bullish.

Contrast this institutional caution with retail behavior: Non-reportables have spiked their S&P 500 long from 63 to 94 and their Dow long from 60 to 96, reaching extremely crowded levels. This cohort consistently serves as a fade at market extremes.

Sectorally, asset managers are cutting exposure to Health Care (100→54) but are now maximally crowded long in Financials (100) and Utilities (96). This suggests a defensive rotation towards safety and value. Leveraged funds are also crowded long Health Care (94), Financials (87), and Communication Services (94), implying a bifurcated tactical trade where some growth-leaning sectors (HC, Comm. Svcs) are still being chased.

Rotation

Capital is flowing into Health Care, Financials, and Communication Services, with Health Care and Communication Services showing sustained leadership. Outflows are concentrated in Energy, Industrials, and Utilities, with Industrials as a sustained laggard. This implies selective pursuit of growth and financials, while broader cyclical and defensive sectors face headwinds. The net outflow from Utilities clashes with asset managers' crowded long Utilities position, suggesting institutional conviction is not yet translating into broader market participation for the sector.

Volatility structure

The VIXEQ/VIX ratio remains deeply in a SYSTEMIC regime, with a z-score of +2.86 (+2.33 current). This extreme dispersion reflects single-stock implied volatility running hot relative to the index, indicating the crowded dispersion trade is stretched and vulnerable. Historically, such conditions precede broad market pullbacks as correlation snaps back.

Dispersion & concentration

The market structure remains firmly in "A Fragile Concentration" structurally and tactically. The spread is high (+33.6 pts), reflecting low correlation and churning single stocks, but the SPX/SPXEW ratio (0.873) signals mega-cap leadership and narrowing breadth. This regime describes a market that is euphoric at the top, driven by a few large names, but is inherently fragile and highly susceptible to a correlation snap-back and sharp liquidation.

Read & caveats

The confluence of institutional distribution into retail capitulation, alongside extreme SYSTEMIC volatility structure and a "Fragile Concentration" market regime, paints a picture of heightened risk. The market is vulnerable to a sharp pullback and correlation snap-back. The primary counter-signal is the continued selective crowding in Health Care, Financials, and Communication Services by leveraged funds and, in the case of Financials and Utilities, asset managers, suggesting not all growth bets are being abandoned. Confirmation of the bearish bias would come from a swift market downdraft, especially if accompanied by a rapid tightening of the VIXEQ/VIX spread.

Regime: DISTRIBUTION. The risk-appetite gauge reads -65 (risk-off).

Each cell is that cohort's net-position percentile vs its own 1Y history (0–100): warm = crowded long (≥90), cool = crowded short (≤10), pale = mid-range.

Signals in play

  • S6 Distribution to Retail — TRIGGERED. Institutions trimming near the highs while retail adds — smart money distributing to the crowd. (now: AM net 4w -42308, AM 60p, SPY 4w +0.2%)
  • S9 Risk Appetite Divergence — WATCH. Small-caps (RTY) lagging while ES stays loved — deteriorating risk appetite, often ahead of a top. (now: RTY 13p, ES 79p, gauge 4w -77)

Rotation

  • Money flowing into: Health Care, Financials, Comm. Svcs
  • Money flowing out of: Energy, Industrials, Utilities
Technology + Communication Services Inflows: Simultaneous strength in these two indicates a concentrated growth/tech-led market (e.g. AI-driven cycle). Staples + Utilities Outflows: This often pairs with the above, reinforcing a shift to risk-on.
Utilities + Health Care Inflows: When both Utilities and Health Care see inflows together, it signals extreme defensiveness – the market is bracing for a downturn or high volatility.

Sentiment

  • AAII bulls 36% / bears 37% (spread -1); NAAIM exposure 83 — Neutral, n/a vs COT
  • Survey sentiment is mid-range (AAII bull-bear -1, NAAIM 83); no contrarian edge.
The AAII Investor Sentiment Survey is a weekly poll of individual (retail) investors asking whether they are bullish, neutral, or bearish on the stock market over the next six months. The headline numbers are the percentage bullish and percentage bearish; the bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish) is the most watched derivative.

Volatility structure & breadth regime

  • Dispersion (VIXEQ vs VIX): ratio 2.69, spread +31.7 pts, z +2.33 → regime SYSTEMIC. Single-stock vol running far above index vol (ratio >1.5σ above its norm) — an extreme, crowded dispersion reading with high mean-reversion potential if the trade unwinds. This is the wide-spread extreme of the scale, not an index crash.
  • Concentration (SPX/SPXEW): 0.863, ratio RSI 44. Crossed with the spread → four-regime map: structural A — Fragile Concentration (tactical A). Flags: spread high. Mega-cap-led index with high single-stock dispersion — euphoric on the surface, fragile underneath; vulnerable to a correlation snap-back.

Both reads come off the same VIXEQ−VIX spread, so they line up: the spread is wide — single-stock vol is running above the index (high dispersion / low correlation), which is the elevated end of the 5-state scale and the top half of the 4-regime map. Treat it as context for the positioning read, not a timing trigger: crowded, mega-cap-tilted futures positioning is far more fragile in a wide-dispersion, concentrated tape (Regime A) than in a broad, healthy one (Regime C).

This map frames the breadth and leadership backdrop for the positioning read. Crowded mega-cap-tilted positioning sitting in Regime A (fragile concentration) is far more dangerous than the same positioning in Regime C (healthy broad bull), where participation is wide.

How to use this

The COT report is published Fridays for the prior Tuesday, so positioning is already a few days stale when read, and it is weekly — it cannot capture intraweek reversals. Positioning also lags price at bottoms: capitulation in the data often prints after the price low.

Sources: caveats, cftc_manual, dispersion_concentration, regimes_and_rotation, sector_rotation, sentiment, volatility_structure.

HOW TO READ THIS REPORT
   p / pct   percentile rank vs a trailing window: 90p = higher than
             90% of past readings (an extreme); 50p = middle; 10p = low.
             e.g. 'Lev gross 1Y 90p' = leveraged-fund gross book is
             larger than 90% of the last year. Sub-headers like
             '(1Y pct, Lev Money net)' mean the numbers are 1-year
             percentiles of that group's net positioning.
   1Y / 3Y   the look-back window: 1Y = last 52 weeks, 3Y = 156 weeks.
   net       long contracts minus short contracts for that group.
   groups    Lev Money = leveraged funds (hedge funds/CTAs);
             AM = asset managers (real money); Non-Rept = small
             traders below reporting limits ('retail').
   states    NEUTRAL = inactive    WATCH = approaching threshold
             TRIGGERED = signal fired
             INFO = informational context only, not a buy/sell trigger
             (e.g. C3 just notes a correlation flipped sign).

Figure 01

Per index (ES=S&P 500, NQ=Nasdaq 100, RTY=Russell 2000, YM=Dow): red = leveraged-fund net contracts, blue = asset-manager net contracts, green/red daily candlesticks = the matching ETF price (right axis; green up-day, red down-day). Watch for funds and price diverging. Footnote: the two dotted lines on each series are its 90th and 10th percentile computed over the last 5 years (config PERCENTILE_WINDOW_YEARS) — a reading outside its band is historically extreme. Because the bands use a 5-year look-back while this chart only plots the last 3 years, a band level can sit outside the visible range, so the lines may not line up with the price/positioning shown here.

Figure 02

Structural regime A — Fragile Concentration: Mega-cap-led index with high single-stock dispersion — euphoric on the surface, fragile underneath; vulnerable to a correlation snap-back. Current spread +31.7 pts, SPX/SPXEW 0.863, ratio RSI 44; tactical (daily) regime A. Institutional-level flags: spread high. The map crosses the VIXEQ−VIX dispersion spread (vertical) with the SPX/SPXEW concentration ratio (horizontal): A fragile-concentration, B late-stage fatigue, C healthy broad bull, D systemic. Axes are z-scores vs each metric's own trailing baseline; the dotted lines mark the playbook's fixed institutional levels. The red × marks Correlation Snap-Back days; the orange ▽ marks Sector Rotation Divergence days. VIXEQ history is young, so regime cutoffs are provisional.

Figure 03

The leveraged-fund book in S&P 500 (ES) split into its raw long and short contracts, plus net (black = long minus short). Both gross sides rising = bigger two-way bets (more fragile); a wide gap or extreme net = a strong directional lean. Footnote: the two dotted lines on each series are its 90th and 10th percentile computed over the last 5 years (config PERCENTILE_WINDOW_YEARS) — a reading outside its band is historically extreme. Because the bands use a 5-year look-back while this chart only plots the last 3 years, a band level can sit outside the visible range, so the lines may not line up with the price/positioning shown here.

Figure 04

The same long / short / net breakdown for asset managers — real-money institutions (pensions, mutual/insurance funds, mostly long-only) in S&P 500 (ES). These are slower, benchmark-driven flows; a falling gross long (or net) near highs is the institutional-distribution tell (signal S6). Footnote: the two dotted lines on each series are its 90th and 10th percentile computed over the last 5 years (config PERCENTILE_WINDOW_YEARS) — a reading outside its band is historically extreme. Because the bands use a 5-year look-back while this chart only plots the last 3 years, a band level can sit outside the visible range, so the lines may not line up with the price/positioning shown here.

Figure 05

The long / short / net breakdown for non-reportables — small traders below CFTC reporting limits, the usual 'retail' proxy — in S&P 500 (ES). Retail net piling in long near highs (or maximally short at lows) is the contrarian/capitulation tell. Footnote: the two dotted lines on each series are its 90th and 10th percentile computed over the last 5 years (config PERCENTILE_WINDOW_YEARS) — a reading outside its band is historically extreme. Because the bands use a 5-year look-back while this chart only plots the last 3 years, a band level can sit outside the visible range, so the lines may not line up with the price/positioning shown here.

Figure 06

Leveraged funds in VIX futures: gross long (green), gross short (red), and net (black = long minus short). Funds are structurally short VIX (harvesting the volatility-risk premium); a deeply negative net = crowded short-vol (complacency), a sharp move toward zero = that crowd covering into fear (signals S3 / S12). Footnote: the two dotted lines on each series are its 90th and 10th percentile computed over the last 5 years (config PERCENTILE_WINDOW_YEARS) — a reading outside its band is historically extreme. Because the bands use a 5-year look-back while this chart only plots the last 3 years, a band level can sit outside the visible range, so the lines may not line up with the price/positioning shown here.

Figure 07

The same gross long / short / net view for non-reportables (retail) in VIX futures. Retail tends to buy VIX protection (net long) into selloffs; extremes can mark sentiment washouts. Footnote: the two dotted lines on each series are its 90th and 10th percentile computed over the last 5 years (config PERCENTILE_WINDOW_YEARS) — a reading outside its band is historically extreme. Because the bands use a 5-year look-back while this chart only plots the last 3 years, a band level can sit outside the visible range, so the lines may not line up with the price/positioning shown here.

Figure 08

Each sector's leveraged-fund positioning percentile minus the S&P 500's, by week. Green = funds more positioned in that sector than the broad index (rotating in); red = rotating out.

Figure 09

This week's flow momentum per sector (4-week change in fund positioning, normalised). Green = money flowing in, red = flowing out. Longer bars = stronger rotation.

Figure 10

How leveraged funds are positioned in interest-rate futures, each as a 1-year percentile (100 = most net-long in a year, 0 = most net-short). ZN = 10-Year US Treasury Note, SOFR = 3-Month SOFR (front-end rate bets), FF = 30-Day Fed Funds (near-term Fed path). Black line (right axis) = the 10-Year Treasury yield. Funds going very short ZN (low percentile) as yields climb is the classic rate-hike lean.

Figure 11

Rolling 13-week correlation between weekly changes in 10-Year Treasury (ZN) positioning and each rate-sensitive sector's positioning. Near +1 = that sector and bonds are being traded together; near -1 = opposite; near 0 = no link. A line crossing zero (a sign flip) means a macro relationship is breaking — that is what signal C3 flags.

Figure 12

Each row is one signal, each column a week (last 26). Cell colour = that signal's state: light grey = neutral, pale amber = info (context only, not a trade trigger), amber = watch / elevated, red = triggered. The black and purple lines are the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 (rebased to 100 at the window start, right axis) so you can line signal flares up against price. Codes are explained in the table below.

Signal code reference

CodeNameWhat it means
Broad-market signals
S1Crowded LongLeveraged funds heavily net-long ES into a calm, rising market — crowded longs that can unwind sharply.
S2Crowded Short ReversalLeveraged funds heavily net-short ES with high VIX and falling open interest — a short base that can squeeze higher.
S3VIX ComplacencyFunds heavily short VIX futures while VIX is low — short-volatility crowding / complacency.
S4CapitulationForced-selling bottom signature: funds maximally short, VIX in panic, open interest collapsing, retail offside.
S4bAlt CapitulationAlternative coincident bottom (sandbox): smart money (asset managers/dealers) buying into a drawdown, or a deep-drawdown washout with funds and retail both short-extreme. Catches slow-grind bottoms S4's 4-of-4 panic gate misses.
S5ExuberanceComposite count of bullish extremes (asset-manager & fund longs, low VIX, short-vol, retail long, plus AAII bulls & NAAIM exposure when available).
S6Distribution to RetailInstitutions trimming near the highs while retail adds — smart money distributing to the crowd.
S7Dry PowderSidelined buying power: asset managers light and funds short means ample fuel; the opposite means depleted.
S8Bottom SequenceMulti-week bottoming lifecycle: capitulation -> stabilization -> asset managers re-accumulating.
S9Risk Appetite DivergenceSmall-caps (RTY) lagging while ES stays loved — deteriorating risk appetite, often ahead of a top.
S10Rally QualityWhether a price move is backed by new money (open interest rising) or just short-covering / liquidation.
S11Gross Exposure FragilityLeveraged gross book at multi-year extremes — crowded two-way exposure that is fragile to shocks.
S12VIX Fear ArrivalThe short-volatility crowd covering rapidly — fear arriving.
Rotation signals
R1StagflationStagflation rotation: into Energy & Materials, out of Consumer Discretionary & Technology.
R2Rate-HikeRate-hike rotation: into Financials, out of Utilities & Real Estate, funds short Treasuries.
R3RecessionRecession rotation: into defensives (Staples, Utilities, Health Care), out of cyclicals, risk-off.
R4ReflationReflation / risk-on rotation: into cyclicals (Discretionary, Industrials, Financials, Materials).
Confluence signals
C1HawkishHawkish confluence: short Treasuries + long Financials + short rate-sensitive sectors line up.
C2DovishDovish confluence: long Treasuries + Utilities + Technology, short Financials line up.
C3Broken RelationshipA rates-sector correlation has flipped sign recently — a macro relationship is breaking.
Figure 13

Total open interest per index as a 1-year percentile (100 = most contracts outstanding in the past year, 0 = fewest) rather than raw millions, so the four indices are directly comparable. Rising = new money/positions building; falling = positions being closed.

Figure 14

Survey sentiment as context for the futures positioning. Top: AAII retail investors bullish (green) / neutral (grey) / bearish (red), % of weekly poll. Bottom: the NAAIM index of active-manager equity exposure (dotted line = the 90 level that counts toward S5 exuberance). These are contrarian at extremes — crowd euphoria/panic often marks turns. Survey sentiment is mid-range (AAII bull-bear -1, NAAIM 83); no contrarian edge.

Appendix

Appendix 01

Per sector: red = leveraged-fund net contracts, blue = asset-manager net contracts (left axis), with the matching sector-ETF daily candles (right axis; green up-day, red down-day) over the trailing 3 years. The sector analogue of the broad-index positioning chart up top — watch for fast money and the ETF price diverging within a sector. Footnote: the two dotted lines on each series are its 90th and 10th percentile computed over the last 5 years (config PERCENTILE_WINDOW_YEARS) — a reading outside its band is historically extreme. Because the bands use a 5-year look-back while this chart only plots the last 3 years, a band level can sit outside the visible range, so the lines may not line up with the price/positioning shown here.

Appendix 02

For S&P 500 (ES) and Nasdaq (NQ): the rolling 90th (solid) and 10th (dotted) percentile of each trader category's NET position, computed over 1-, 2- and 3-year trailing windows — 6 lines per panel, coloured by window. Shows how the extremes of positioning have drifted over the full history: a band edge rising over time means that category's net is ranging higher than it used to; the latest net sitting at a band edge is historically extreme for that look-back. Wider windows (3Y) give slower, broader bands; 1Y reacts fastest.

Appendix 03

The full stored history of each survey series, so the latest weekly reading sits in decades of context. Top: AAII retail bulls / neutral / bears (% of poll). Middle: the NAAIM active-manager equity-exposure index (dotted line = the 90 level that feeds S5). Bottom: the AAII Asset Allocation Survey stock / bond / cash mix (% of portfolio). NAAIM and the allocation survey are seeded from history and appended each week.

Appendix 04

Volatility structure: the VIX (index implied vol, blue) vs the VIXEQ (Cboe S&P 500 Constituent index — single-stock implied vol, red). VIXEQ sits structurally above VIX; the signal is how STRETCHED the VIXEQ/VIX ratio is vs its own trailing baseline — the z-score (black, middle) mapped onto the five labelled regime bands (CONCENTRATION → NORMAL → ELEVATED → DISPERSION → SYSTEMIC at >1.5σ); the star marks today's regime and the bottom ribbon is the regime each day over time. A wide spread (grey dotted) marks a crowded dispersion trade — index vol sold against single-stock vol — whose unwind has historically preceded broad pullbacks. Here SYSTEMIC means an extreme-DISPERSION reading (single-stock vol far above index vol), not an index crash. VIXEQ history begins Nov 2024, so the baseline is young and the band cutoffs are provisional.

Appendix 05

Market breadth: the cap-weighted S&P 500 (^GSPC) divided by the equal-weighted S&P 500 (^SPXEW), with each index's daily candles below. A rising ratio = mega-caps outperforming the average stock (narrowing breadth); falling = broadening participation.

How to read the breadth ratio (^GSPC ÷ ^SPXEW)

Rising = cap-weighted index outperforming the equal-weighted index (mega-caps leading). Falling = the average stock outperforming the giants.

1. Rising ratio — ^GSPC outperforming ^SPXEW

  • Narrow market breadth: headline gains are driven by a very small group of massive companies (the "Magnificent Seven" / heavy tech weights); the rest of the market may be flat or declining.
  • Clustered momentum: strong momentum and institutional flows focusing on growth, secular themes (AI adoption), or safe-haven large caps with pristine balance sheets and strong free-cash-flow margins.
  • Concentration risk: a multi-year rising ratio flags structural risk — when the top 10 names approach historic concentration (e.g. nearing 40% of the index), the headline index becomes highly vulnerable to idiosyncratic shocks from just a few tickers, even if the economy is fine.

2. Falling ratio — ^SPXEW outperforming ^GSPC

  • Broad market participation: the average stock outperforming the top-heavy giants — typically healthier; cyclicals (Industrials, Financials, Materials) and mid/large caps catching bids.
  • Economic expansion & rotation: often coincides with value rotation, an improving economic outlook, or mean reversion when the mega-cap valuation gap stretches too thin.
  • Defensive or value focus: equal-weight's quarterly rebalance (selling recent winners, buying laggards) gives it a natural value tilt; outperformance here reflects a shift from pure momentum toward broader fundamentals.
Ratio trendMarket meaningKey driversTypical phase
RisingNarrowing breadth, high concentration, momentum-driven Mega-cap tech/growth outperformanceLate-stage bull market or sector bubbles (e.g. AI rush)
FallingBroadening participation, healthy market structure Cyclicals, value, and mid-tier large caps outperformingEarly-to-mid recovery, or valuation mean-reversion

Monitoring this ratio helps gauge whether a rally has "good breadth" or a handful of tech giants are artificially holding up an otherwise weak tape.

Signal reference — states & trigger conditions

Each signal's possible statuses and the exact condition for each. "p" = percentile rank over the trailing window (1Y unless noted); "held persistence" = the condition must hold for the signal's persistence_weeks consecutive reports. Thresholds shown are the config defaults.

CodeNameWhat it meansStatusCondition for that status
S1Crowded LongLeveraged funds heavily net-long ES into a calm, rising market — crowded longs that can unwind sharply.TRIGGEREDES lev-money net >=90p AND VIX <16 AND SPY 4-week return >0 (held persistence)
WATCHES lev-money net in 80-90p
NEUTRALotherwise
S2Crowded Short ReversalLeveraged funds heavily net-short ES with high VIX and falling open interest — a short base that can squeeze higher.TRIGGEREDES lev net <=10p AND VIX >22 AND open interest fell over the last 2 weeks (held persistence)
WATCHES lev net in 10-20p
NEUTRALotherwise
S3VIX ComplacencyFunds heavily short VIX futures while VIX is low — short-volatility crowding / complacency.TRIGGEREDleveraged short-VIX position >=90p AND VIX <15
WATCHleveraged short-VIX position in 80-90p
NEUTRALotherwise
S4CapitulationForced-selling bottom signature: funds maximally short, VIX in panic, open interest collapsing, retail offside.TRIGGEREDcore = 4/4 of {lev net <=5p; VIX >30; ES OI 4-week change <-10%; retail net short or <=5p} (held persistence)
WATCHcore >=2 of those 4
NEUTRALcore <2
S4bAlt CapitulationAlternative coincident bottom (sandbox): smart money (asset managers/dealers) buying into a drawdown, or a deep-drawdown washout with funds and retail both short-extreme. Catches slow-grind bottoms S4's 4-of-4 panic gate misses.TRIGGEREDABSORPTION [(asset-mgr accumulation z>=1 OR dealer-covering z>=1) AND SPY drawdown <=-8%] OR WASHOUT [drawdown-intensity z>=1.5 AND ES lev net <=15p AND retail net <=25p]
NEUTRALneither archetype present
S5ExuberanceComposite count of bullish extremes (asset-manager & fund longs, low VIX, short-vol, retail long, plus AAII bulls & NAAIM exposure when available).EXUBERANT>=4 of {AM net >=95p; lev net >=90p; VIX <14; short-VIX >=90p; retail net >=90p; AAII bulls >50%; NAAIM exposure >90} (last two optional, only when sentiment data present)
INFOfewer than 4 (informational count, never a trade trigger)
S6Distribution to RetailInstitutions trimming near the highs while retail adds — smart money distributing to the crowd.TRIGGEREDAM net falling over 4 weeks AND SPY >=95% of its high AND retail net rising over 4 weeks (held persistence)
WATCHAM net falling 4w AND AM net 4 weeks ago >=70p AND SPY 4-week return <=0
NEUTRALotherwise
S7Dry PowderSidelined buying power: asset managers light and funds short means ample fuel; the opposite means depleted.AMPLEAM net <30p AND leveraged gross-short >=80p (lots of sidelined buying power)
DEPLETEDAM net >85p AND leveraged gross-short <=20p (buying power spent)
MODERATEotherwise
S8Bottom SequenceMulti-week bottoming lifecycle: capitulation -> stabilization -> asset managers re-accumulating.DURABLE_BOTTOM_CONFIRMEDphase-A present recently AND asset-mgr net rose 3 consecutive weeks
BOTTOM_SIGNATURE_LIKELYphase-A present: S4 fired in the last 6w, lev net recovered >30p from below 5p, VIX compressed from a >30 panic
NEUTRALotherwise
S9Risk Appetite DivergenceSmall-caps (RTY) lagging while ES stays loved — deteriorating risk appetite, often ahead of a top.TRIGGEREDall 4 of {RTY lev net falling 3 weeks; RTY <50p; ES >70p; risk-appetite gauge 4-week change <-25} (held persistence)
WATCH>=3 of those 4
NEUTRALotherwise
S10Rally QualityWhether a price move is backed by new money (open interest rising) or just short-covering / liquidation.REAL_MONEYSPY 4-week return >+2% AND ES OI 4-week change >+5% (rally backed by new money)
SHORT_COVERSPY >+2% AND OI <-5% (rally on short-covering)
DISTRIBUTIONSPY <-2% AND OI >+5% (selloff with new shorts/positions)
LIQUIDATIONSPY <-2% AND OI <-5% (positions being closed)
NEUTRALmoves within +/-2% / +/-5%
S11Gross Exposure FragilityLeveraged gross book at multi-year extremes — crowded two-way exposure that is fragile to shocks.TRIGGEREDleveraged gross >=90p (1Y) AND >=85p (3Y) (held persistence)
WATCHleveraged gross >=80p (1Y) OR >=80p (3Y)
NEUTRALotherwise
S12VIX Fear ArrivalThe short-volatility crowd covering rapidly — fear arriving.TRIGGERED(short-VIX week-over-week <=-25% OR VIX net-change percentile <=5) AND prior-week short-VIX >=70p (held persistence)
WATCHshort-VIX wow <=-15% AND prior-week short-VIX >=60p
NEUTRALotherwise
R1StagflationStagflation rotation: into Energy & Materials, out of Consumer Discretionary & Technology.TRIGGERED4/4 of {Energy >=80p; Materials >=80p; Cons. Disc. <=20p; Technology <=20p} (held 2 weeks)
WATCH>=2 of those 4
NEUTRALotherwise
R2Rate-HikeRate-hike rotation: into Financials, out of Utilities & Real Estate, funds short Treasuries.TRIGGERED4/4 of {Financials >=80p; Utilities <=20p; Real Estate <=20p; 10Y (ZN) <=20p} (held 2 weeks)
WATCH>=2 of those 4
NEUTRALotherwise
R3RecessionRecession rotation: into defensives (Staples, Utilities, Health Care), out of cyclicals, risk-off.TRIGGERED>=6 of {Staples >=80p; Utilities >=80p; Health Care >=80p; Cons. Disc. <=20p; Industrials <=20p; risk gauge <=-30} (held 2 weeks)
WATCH>=3 of those
NEUTRALotherwise
R4ReflationReflation / risk-on rotation: into cyclicals (Discretionary, Industrials, Financials, Materials).TRIGGERED>=5 of {Cons. Disc. >=80p; Industrials >=80p; Financials >=80p; Materials >=80p; risk gauge >=+30} (held 2 weeks)
WATCH>=3 of those
NEUTRALotherwise
C1HawkishHawkish confluence: short Treasuries + long Financials + short rate-sensitive sectors line up.TRIGGERED10Y (ZN) <=10p AND Financials >=90p AND (Utilities <=10p OR Real Estate <=10p)
NEUTRALotherwise
C2DovishDovish confluence: long Treasuries + Utilities + Technology, short Financials line up.TRIGGERED10Y (ZN) >=90p AND Utilities >=90p AND Technology >=90p AND Financials <=10p
NEUTRALotherwise
C3Broken RelationshipA rates-sector correlation has flipped sign recently — a macro relationship is breaking.INFOany rates-sector 13-week correlation flipped sign vs 4 weeks ago (macro relationship breaking)
NEUTRALno sign flip
